title: Clustering

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Clustering; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: scikit-learn User Guide; 1.9.x target, accessed 2026-07; User Guide sections 2, 3.4.7, and 7.5: clustering, novelty/outlier detection, decomposition, and evaluation; atomic topic: Clustering
sources.claim: Clustering groups examples according to a chosen representation, distance or density model, and algorithm parameters; quality is relative to the intended exploratory or operational use rather than hidden true labels by default.

sources.claim: Clustering groups examples according to a chosen representation, distance or density model, and algorithm parameters; quality is relative to the intended exploratory or operational use rather than hidden true labels by default.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Clustering groups examples according to a chosen representation, distance or density model, and algorithm parameters; quality is relative to the intended exploratory or operational use rather than hidden true labels by default.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Numeric features are scaled, a k-means result is compared across k and seeds, and clusters are described by original features without treating numbers as discovered human categories. Your goal is to apply and critique a clustering result under at least two parameter or representation choices. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Data and experimental workflow; Bias, variance and regularization; Features and preprocessing. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Clustering groups examples according to a chosen representation, distance or density model, and algorithm parameters; quality is relative to the intended exploratory or operational use rather than hidden true labels by default. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Scaling changes distances, k-means favors centroid-shaped groups, density methods depend on neighborhood parameters, hierarchical cuts are choices, and visually separated clusters may be unstable or meaningless. The learner must reject this overclaim: A clustering algorithm discovers the natural categories already present in data, and a high silhouette score proves those categories are real.

includes: Compare centroid, density and hierarchical concepts and the role of scaling and distance.
Apply and critique a clustering result under at least two parameter or representation choices.
excludes: Scaling changes distances, k-means favors centroid-shaped groups, density methods depend on neighborhood parameters, hierarchical cuts are choices, and visually separated clusters may be unstable or meaningless.

outcomes: Apply and critique a clustering result under at least two parameter or representation choices.

examples.title: Clustering: bounded direct case
examples.situation: Numeric features are scaled, a k-means result is compared across k and seeds, and clusters are described by original features without treating numbers as discovered human categories.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: Numeric features are scaled, a k-means result is compared across k and seeds, and clusters are described by original features without treating numbers as discovered human categories.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Treat clusters as representation- and parameter-dependent summaries; test stability, sensitivity, usefulness, and harms before assigning real-world meaning.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Treat clusters as representation- and parameter-dependent summaries; test stability, sensitivity, usefulness, and harms before assigning real-world meaning.

examples.title: Clustering: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A density method marks sparse points as noise under one radius but changes sharply under another, so sensitivity and domain review are reported.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A density method marks sparse points as noise under one radius but changes sharply under another, so sensitivity and domain review are reported.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Scaling changes distances, k-means favors centroid-shaped groups, density methods depend on neighborhood parameters, hierarchical cuts are choices, and visually separated clusters may be unstable or meaningless.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: A clustering algorithm discovers the natural categories already present in data, and a high silhouette score proves those categories are real.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Apply and critique a clustering result under at least two parameter or representation choices.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: A clustering algorithm discovers the natural categories already present in data, and a high silhouette score proves those categories are real.

analogy.example: an exploratory sorting table whose representation, distance, projection, reference population, and exposure policy determine visible patterns can introduce Clustering only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.

limitations: algorithmic groups and outliers are parameter-dependent summaries rather than discovered human categories or harms

faded.prompt: Quick check for “Clustering: bounded direct case”: state its starting evidence, apply the Clustering rule, and give the observable result in three short lines.
scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

recap.summary: You should now be able to apply and critique a clustering result under at least two parameter or representation choices. Keep this boundary in view: Scaling changes distances, k-means favors centroid-shaped groups, density methods depend on neighborhood parameters, hierarchical cuts are choices, and visually separated clusters may be unstable or meaningless.
